channel.title: posts on istvn.urbn
channel.description: Recent content in posts on istvn.urbn

item.title: down the coffee spiral
item.description: I purchased my first proper coffee grinder in 2019 in the form of a Porlex Tall. I solely based my decision on the great video that James Hoffmann did on comparing budget hand grinders at that time. Towards the end of 2022, the taste started to get hollow.
item.content:encoded:
I purchased my first proper coffee grinder in 2019 in the form of a Porlex Tall. I solely based my decision on the great video that James Hoffmann did on comparing budget hand grinders at that time. Towards the end of 2022, the taste started to get hollow.

gear acquisition syndrome ⚙️

Speaking about that video, I started to watch more and more coffee equipment-related content, which resulted in a desire to purchase something nice. First, it was ceramic cups from Sebesi Ceramics, followed by a cutting/serving board from Vinovita.

Remember that part in the introduction where I noted that the taste became hollow? That feeling became more prominent after each visit to a café. I had a nice cup of coffee, purchased the same beans, and prepared with the same method at home, but the taste wasn’t as good. So, I went ahead and decided to buy a top-of-the-line hand grinder. The research happened quickly, and I went ahead and ordered a 1Zpresso K-Max unit. After around a month of use, I can say that it was an excellent decision.

I A/B tested the old and the new grinder with the same coffee and method with one of my friends. We concluded that we would be happier if the difference wouldn’t be this stark. It is not a surprise, as the K-MAX’s grind size is as uniform as it gets. It produces more fines than I would like it to do so, but I can take that tradeoff any day of the week, as the flavors are excellent. I felt the urge to tell my brother about my finding because he happens to be a food engineer. He made me realize that he had written his thesis on the importance of particle size in powder mixtures. So, it wasn’t a surprise for him. Hopefully, my honeymoon with my new grinder will last longer than my memory, as I don’t want to invest more in this hobby soon. However, I have some ideas about my upcoming investments… 😅

starting a cult ⛪

At the end of last year, I took my V60 to the office to prepare a nice cup of coffee after lunch. These items are alien in the kitchen areas of offices where automatic coffee machines prevail. At first, everyone thought that I was the crazy person around here. Since then, months have passed. In those months, I received numerous questions about the coffee and the method as the whole séance became an attraction. More curious colleagues asked whether they could have a taste. As usual, this was the first step on a slippery slope.

Fast forward a few months, and this ritual of mine became a base for exploring different types of coffee beans and socializing in the office. Our company has a very relaxed home office policy, so it was hard for many to feel a sense of belonging after things started to get back to normal after the COVID times. I had that feeling too. It fills me with joy that I can contribute in my way of bringing the team together. I’m enjoying coffee more since the whole thing started to develop, and my colleagues enjoy the coffee and the conversations around it. Even my supervisor noted that I have a cult now, as we occupy the main table in the kitchen area.

Giving back to the community helps me have fun in my hobby in a completely different way than before. It pushes me to play my best game and help others to explore new things. It also made me realize that I like it when I have a buzz around me, but being the center of attention still freaks me out.

item.title: way to boshan
item.description: Being an avid podcast listener for a decade has the consequence of suppressing it to the background and not paying that much attention to content. Luckily, this time I was in an exploration mood and came across a conversation with Tamás Sajó that triggered something.
item.content:encoded:
Being an avid podcast listener for a decade has the consequence of suppressing it to the background and not paying that much attention to content. Luckily, this time I was in an exploration mood and came across a conversation with Tamás Sajó that triggered something.

Apart from being extremely knowledgeable, Sajó is the most humble scholar I’ve ever heard. He can speak about any topic because of his vast knowledge while doing it with the utmost respect. He is an art historian by trade who worked at the Hungarian Academy Of Sciences, and teaches about middle age history and applied information sciences at CEU. After breaking free from the academy, he founded his own publishing company, Studiolum. He translates books, holds online seminars, and organizes thematic trips. According to the “Az élet, meg minden” podcast, he speaks at least 15 (fifteen!) languages and can make himself understood by 15 others.

In one interview, he talked about a Chinese poem by Xin Qiji and the intricacies of translating these poems into any other language. The piece itself and his interpretation both struck me. Before jumping to conclusions, here is the poem itself in Chinese and English side-by-side:

Original English1
醜奴兒; Tune: Chou Nu Er
書博山道中壁 Written on the Wall on My Way to Boshan
少年不識愁滋味， While young, I knew no grief I could not bear
愛上層樓。 I’d like to go upstair
愛上層樓， I’d like to go upstair
為賦新詞強說愁。 To write new verses with a false despair
而今識盡愁滋味， I know what grief is now that I am old
欲說還休。 I would not have it told
欲說還休， I would not have it told
卻道天涼好個秋。 But only say I’m glad that autumn’s cold

The tension between the two verses culminates at the end of them. It comes from the similarity and stark contrast between autumn (秋, qiū) and despair (愁, chóu). Fall is the season of burnbaiting, and that is what its logogram represents. Reaching the end of the natural cadence of things, we come to a clean - yet calm - state. On the other hand, we have autumn’s symbol expanded with the heart (心, xīn) character, which altogether means sadness. The first verse is about the “false despair” of young age and the willingness to force oneself to write about it, while the second speaks about the pure admiration of a cold autumn without the need to talk about anything else. Maybe this is Xin Qiji’s way of distancing himself from the kitsch of poetry written about false feelings and the inexperienced younger self. Who can blame him? Life is about steering clear of bloat and focusing on what matters.

About ten years ago, I wrote “poems” about sadness, but in retrospect, they speak about false despair. I’m going through a lot of self-reflection lately, which has the same conclusion as the words of Xin Qiji. Maybe that’s why they say that Chinese poems are pinpoint accurate.

PS.: I feel obliged to quote the Hungarian version as well:

Original Hungarian2
醜奴兒; A „Csúnya rabszolga” dallamára.
書博山道中壁 Boshan felé menet egy falra írtam.
少年不識愁滋味， Fiatalon nem ismertem még a bánat ízét,
愛上層樓。 a toronyba vágytam.
愛上層樓， A toronyba vágytam,
為賦新詞強說愁。 hogy képzelt bánatomról verset írjak.
而今識盡愁滋味， Mára a bánat minden ízét megismertem, de már
欲說還休。 nem vágyom beszélni róla.
欲說還休， Nem vágyom beszélni róla,
卻道天涼好個秋。 csak annyit mondok: milyen szép, hideg ősz.

  1. Translation by Xu Yuanchong. Taken from Yuan Jiayin’s and Wang Feng’s article in the European Journal of Applied Linguistics Studies, Volume 5, Issue 1. ↩︎

  2. Translation by Tamás Sajó. A more detailed analysis can be found on his dedicated site. ↩︎

item.title: quarter of debloat
item.description: Sometimes I feel the itch to clean up and debloat certain aspects of my life. I’m not a die-hard purist or a minimalist, but this itch became unbearable in the past few months, so I decided to do something about it. There is no better time to do these activities than at the beginning of the new year. So, here is the list I would like to tackle.
item.content:encoded:
Sometimes I feel the itch to clean up and debloat certain aspects of my life. I’m not a die-hard purist or a minimalist, but this itch became unbearable in the past few months, so I decided to do something about it. There is no better time to do these activities than at the beginning of the new year. So, here is the list I would like to tackle.

photos 🖼

My photo backup strategy before 2015 was rudimental. I had a folder for every year with a subfolder for “significant” events and unsorted pictures. This was stored on the hard drive of my then-Windows-based laptop. As you would expect, the said hard drive failed, rendering photos predating 2005 inaccessible. Moving to Mac in 2015 and subscribing to iCloud storage was a blessing, as this collection is one of my most valuable assets.

The library itself is more or less well-organized. Newly taken shots are cherry-picked, so only the best ones are kept. The case is quite the opposite of the old ones, though. There are a lot of near-identical, blurry, and poor-quality ones. Most of them hold sentimental value. It is a curated collection, so the poor ones need to go. Currently, the library has 17 172 photos and 2 215 videos with a cumulative size of 156,5 GB. It will be a hell of an exercise to weed them out.

Adding insult to injury, I decided to add location data to the pre-iPhone-era pictures. Of the 17 thousand above 3 753 do not have GPS data, and 401 of the 22 hundred is also lacking that. Luckily, I have HashPhotos for the rescue, and I scheduled this exercise for the bi-weekly office commute. I’ll report on how that went, but it won’t be any time soon.

files, apps & services 💾

Comically enough, the external hard drive I used in the past years died on me after I moved to my new computer. So, documents and miscellaneous files live on my laptop or some cloud storage, which is far from optimal. This is an opportunity to consolidate this data and move as much as possible to an offline repository. It will have a better structure and will occupy less space. At the same time, I will achieve the goal of moving my stuff off of privacy-invasive services.

After the cleansing, I have to develop a better backup strategy and reconsider the choice of applications I use. As a FOSS enthusiast, I will do my best to look for a viable open-source alternative to the software I currently use. I will also revisit the options I have in the cases where open source is not an option. The goal is to use more privacy-respecting and self-sovereign tools without hidden fees. Maybe in a future post, I will go through my stack once it is stabilized.

I checked how many entries were in my password manager. The number is too damn high, considering how many web services I use regularly. Most of them are just simple registration for services I was curious about, while the other half is replaceable or duplicate. Gaining traction with the above cleaning projects, it is safe to say that I can weed out this particular part of my digital life.

Maybe everything above is a huge pile, but I still plan on shaving it down in Q1.

item.title: after the snap
item.description: A month has passed since my last post, and conveniently enough we slipped into 2023. With the new year new me approach, I can have a fresh start with some reservations. I think that is bullshit, but I’m in better shape mentally to change things up.
item.content:encoded:
A month has passed since my last post, and conveniently enough we slipped into 2023. With the new year new me approach, I can have a fresh start with some reservations. I think that is bullshit, but I’m in better shape mentally to change things up.

blogging 📰

I came across an article on The Verge, that nudged me in the direction to continue this blog. I’m not 100% aligned with all the points made by the author, but it resonates with me. I’m very far from all the Twitter craze that happens right now, so I’m not on the lookout for shelter. However, I’m carving my space out on the web that can be my creation.

self-care 💊

As I stated in my previous post, I feel that I’m a bit stuck. I think it is changing for the better, as my employer gave me positive feedback. We even talked about an opportunity to move forward professionally. So, there is a path ahead.

I’m a religious user of YNAB, but lately, I’ve been using it as an expense tracker rather than a budgeting tool. This also means that I’m not conscious of my finances. So, the projects on that front are replacing YNAB with a self-hosted solution, utilizing the Interactive Brokers’ account that I have to accumulate some savings, considering the pension-related incentive offered by my employer, and setting up budgeting goals to keep myself in check. I think that can be achieved in the 1st quarter.

Reading was a huge part of my daily routine. In the past two years, I neglected it. Around the 4th quarter of 2022, I picked up my Kindle again and started to read every morning before work. That helped me to go through my backlog of saved articles. I strive to read more, so I have to take time out from other activities.

That activity will be social media use as it is less fun than ever. Also, the privacy invasion that all the major platforms do is very offputting. I was an avid user of Facebook and Instagram, but I became more and more skeptical about them. To preserve my sanity, I explicitly keep myself away from the TikToks and BeReals of the world. I also started to follow privacy-focused advocates and some of their points flipped something in me. Maybe, that topic deserves a post of its own.

In the past decade, I was very focused on being physically fit. The changes I implemented in my diet towards the end of 2022 started to push me to the form I strived for. However, I still have to improve on some fronts. I have to seek professional help, which needs some courage.

All the above implies that this year will be about clearing stuff out, reinforcing the foundations, focusing on things that matter, and embracing change that can make me better. What I have now feels like an acceptable start, but there is always room for improvement.

technical stuff 🔧

It is evident now, that I like tinkering around with the blog’s framework more than actually writing posts. I canceled my web hosting contract when I realized that I can host this site on GitHub Pages. After changing the theme and making minor touch-ups on the posts, I cut ties with the Vox Scurra moniker for good. It feels better to post all of these under my name rather than a pseudonym.

The only thing left to do is secure a domain with my name and continue the lost art of blogging.

item.title: marginal success
item.description: Recently I’ve received a notification from my hosting provider that the yearly installment is due for payment on the 20th of December. This fact got me thinking, so here is the deal.
item.content:encoded:
Recently I’ve received a notification from my hosting provider that the yearly installment is due for payment on the 20th of December. This fact got me thinking, so here is the deal.

Honestly, I’m not achieving what I intended to do when I started this website. I had vivid dreams about posting at least biweekly and how much it will help me to articulate my thoughts. There were times when I enjoyed writing posts but other times it was quite the opposite. It felt like a burden. There were no KPIs attached to this but still, I am not that satisfied.

The above described is a common theme with me recently. I do not feel successful or do not feel supported in the things I do. So, I have the following options to go forward:

  1. Continue to have this site as-is without expectations from myself.
  2. Change things up and relaunch the site under a new moniker and different tooling. (I came to dislike the Vox Scurra name anyway 😅)
  3. Write this blog to me in Markdown files without ever publishing them.
  4. Stop doing all of this.

I’m leaning toward option two. I enjoy tinkering with the underlying system, sometimes more than writing posts. I also think that new things tend to change rapidly in the beginning. This website is no exception either.

item.title: v60 is the way
item.description: More than a month has passed since I’ve written about keeping an offline log of the coffees I try, and I might put some of it on this blog. Good news, the first time is now!
item.content:encoded:
More than a month has passed since I’ve written about keeping an offline log of the coffees I try, and I might put some of it on this blog. Good news, the first time is now!

meeting the parents

On our recent trip to Slovenia, we visited Stow Coffee in Ljubjana by accident. It was a smashing decision as they had Coffea eugenioides on their filter menu. The price was steep at €20, but I let the barista persuade me to try it.

So what is so special about it?

  • Along with Coffea canephora, they are the parents of the beloved Coffea arabica.
  • It is considered by some to be an abandoned species.
  • It is inherently sweet and boasts a unique set of flavor characteristics.

It was unassuming at first but had some raw cereal/hay notes. The taste was light and reminded me of porridge with some mild sweetness. The aftertaste was the bomb, as fruity flavors started to dominate that lasted. That lasting flavor part was the one that got me, which is the measure of quality.

I discussed this experience with the owner of Szeged’s iconic Kék Elefánt café, who remarked that the hay note might either be a species-specific thing or a sign of lighter than ideal roast. If you have the chance, try it but do not prepare yourself for an earth-shattering experience.

the proper method

Speaking of the café in Szeged, I had a delightful cup of Peruvian coffee there. I had to ask them what method are they using to prepare the filter drinks and what coffee are they using. After purchasing one bag of beans, I decided to pull my V60 out of the depths of the cupboard. Using it is more involved than the Aeropress, considering you are like me, who follows James Hoffman’s ultimate guide.

At first, I was not getting the same flavor profile as I did in the café. That baffled me, so I stepped over my laziness and started tweaking the grind size on my Porlex. After 3-4 tries, I got what I wanted. I also tested it with different coffees that were available at home, and I have to say I’ve cracked it for myself.

These two episodes made me realize a few things. You must push yourself to experiment with different methods and dare to set that grinder finer/coarser in larger increments. Fiddling with the V60 reconnected me with the coffee preparation process, so I decided to put the Aeropress into the travel kit for now.

item.title: platform of choice
item.description: After deciding to restart blogging, my first task was to look for a simple yet powerful platform.
item.content:encoded:
After deciding to restart blogging, my first task was to look for a simple yet powerful platform.

pepperidge farm remembers 👴

In prehistoric times, I hand-coded my homepage with a text editor. God, the end product looked horrible, and it wasn’t fun. So, I switched to a less labor-intensive publishing tool, WordPress. That had the benefits of being customizable and light yet feature-rich enough for my needs.

Fast forward a decade, and the standard on the internet changed dramatically. The median page weight on desktops climbed from 738,3 Kilobytes to 2 291,6 Kilobytes in ten years. Mobile is even worse as the starting median was nearly half of the desktop’s figure while the ending is only 200 Kilobytes lower. The main drivers are reliance on CDNs, embedded content, extensive tracking, general asset size, and lack of optimization.

In short, the web is bloated.

enter hugo 🐭

As a minimalist and pragmatic person, I said no to all the nonsense described above. I ended up choosing a “static site generator” that checks the following boxes for me:

  • Simple to use and maintain
  • Flexible and customizable
  • Modern web ready
  • Actively developed

The final decision was to use Hugo with the PaperMod theme. It does not just meet my expectations but over-delivers in meaningful ways:

  • It’s an open-source project with a thriving community and frequent updates.
  • Currently, the blog is less than 700 Kilobytes in size.
  • Rendering and publishing take less than 2 seconds.
  • You can use it with the version control system of your choice, as it’s a configuration file in YAML, and the content is in Markdown.
  • A GitHub repository and a simple GitHub Action make it portable and CI/CD ready.
  • I must research to utilize all of its features.

You might say it is missing essential features like statistics or comments. I’m not interested in statistics and will not implement it at the expense of tracking. Turning on comments would make the experience a bit more interactive. However, I’m not feeling comfortable with the idea right now, but that might change in the future.

verdict 🔨

My recent addition of version tracking and automatic deployment via GitHub Actions required me to learn and tinker around. By optimizing the workflow, I have one less excuse to avoid posting more.

So, Hugo is the platform for me to move forward.

item.title: kotty
item.description: I visit local cafés at least two times a month and usually purchase beans from Hungarian micro roasteries. I was mostly interested in filter roasts but recently I’ve stumbled upon a blend specifically for the Moka pot in unusual packaging.
item.content:encoded:
I visit local cafés at least two times a month and usually purchase beans from Hungarian micro roasteries. I was mostly interested in filter roasts but recently I’ve stumbled upon a blend specifically for the Moka pot in unusual packaging.

After a while, I decided to give it a try and purchased one can. As far as I know, Lucky Cap Roastery is the first one from Hungary that has a plantation in Lourdes, Costa Rica. Coffee coming from that place goes under the “Bányai” branding and the above-mentioned one is no exception.

They have very specific instructions on how to prepare it, even down to the filtered water that you should use. I already prepare drinks in the Moka pot in a very specific way so this was no surprise, but it brought a smile to my face nonetheless.

To be honest, I’m always skeptical about pre-ground coffee, but this was one of my top five drinks made with that method. They promise a spicy dark chocolate taste with subtle dried plum undertones. I definitely felt the first but the fruitiness was absent. This is most probably due to the fact, that my taste perception is far from ideal and I do not really go above and beyond on taste profiling.

Anyway, this endeavor triggered something in me to be more conscious about the whole coffee experience and it is better to articulate my thoughts than to keep them to myself. That was one of the reasons I decided to start blogging again. In light of the above, I repurposed my Moleskine notebook into a diary where I will keep a log of the coffees I taste and my opinion on them. Some of that might end up here as well.

kotty
Short for kotyogós, the Hungarian nickname of the Moka pot.

item.title: system upgrade
item.content:encoded:
prelude 📜

It was 2015 when I purchased my first Apple machine in form of a MacBook Pro and said farewell to Windows in my private life for good. This platform — macOS — is a good place for me to have a UNIX system. I can play around with the terminal, use as much open-source software as I can, and still have the convenience that the Cupertino company provides.

Fast forward seven years and the honeymoon with my beloved MacBook Pro is well over. I started to notice that it is slower than it used to be. I wanted to switch to a new machine earlier, but the omission of ports, the presence of the touch bar, and the infamous butterfly keyboard held me back. Meanwhile, Apple started to transition to ARM with their M-series of chips, and with the 2021 MacBook Pro line, they returned to the form that I like.

the upgrade 🚀

After waiting nearly four months to see the reaction from the community I decided that it is time for me to upgrade. Speccing it was no easy feat for me, as I like to overthink. Finally, I landed on a 14" MacBook Pro with M1 Pro chip (10 core CPU, 16 core GPU, and 16 GB RAM) with 1TB of storage. Luckily for me, a local retailer run a sale where I was able to purchase the silver one with about a USD 200 discount compared to the Space Grey model.

MacBook Pro (13-inch Retina Early 2015) MacBook Pro (14-inch, 2021)
Model MacBookPro12,1 MacBookPro18,3
CPU Intel Core i5-5257U Apple M1 Pro (10 core)
RAM 8 GB 16 GB
GPU Intel Iris Graphics 6100 Apple M1 Pro (16 core)
Storage 256 GB 1 TB

some figures 📈

From the feel of it, this thing is a beast. But I wanted to be objective so I ran 3 tests 5 times — yes, you read it correctly FIVE times — and compared the averaged results.

The most demanding stuff I do is working with huge files in the Affinity suite of apps. Affinity Photo has a built-in benchmarking tool. They state that the test performance can be translated to real-life workloads and the results are linearly comparable (double the score means twice as fast). I’ve omitted the Raster (Multi GPU) and the Combined (Multi GPU) tests, as these machines do not have multiple GPUs.

Intel MacBook M1 MacBook Gain
Vector (Single CPU) 169 527 +212%
Vector (Multi CPU) 447 3 275 +633%
Raster (Multi CPU) 120 914 +661%
Raster (Single GPU) 2 373 16 852 +610%
Combined (Multi CPU) 144 944 +555%
Combined (Single GPU) 1 760 15 843 +800%

I also help with the distribution of podcasts on YouTube. For that, I use ffmpeg to combine WAV files with PNG files into a video. This is just a simple one-liner that I run in the terminal. Depending on the length of the given episode it can take some time to finish.

Intel MacBook M1 MacBook Gain
ffmpeg 5:36,75 1:16,20 +342%

Recently I learned about Blender Open Data so for a synthetic test, it was a good pick. They have a command-line version which made my life easier. I did it for the fun of it but the results are interesting. I might have to learn 3D modeling after this!

Intel MacBook M1 MacBook Gain
monster 16,413701 98,231952 +498%
junkshop 5,568315 53,057263 +853%
classroom 7,267270 41,592097 +472%

The gains are insane and my old Intel-based MacBook Pro was left in the dust.

conclusion 🔥

I’m very happy with my purchase so far. The port selection is as good as it gets in 2022, the keyboard feels good, and the screen is phenomenal. I have no complaint about performance. I only have had this laptop since yesterday, so I had no time to test the battery life. I think it is a good investment but we will see whether it has the longevity as my past one.

item.title: init
item.description: In the past – which was like 20 years ago – I had a personal website hosted on UltraWEB’s free offering. It was a place where I was able to tinker with stuff learned in Computer Science class and express my thoughts in an unrestricted but still structural way.
item.content:encoded:
In the past – which was like 20 years ago – I had a personal website hosted on UltraWEB’s free offering. It was a place where I was able to tinker with stuff learned in Computer Science class and express my thoughts in an unrestricted but still structural way.

I decided to start something of a blog again with the following goals:

  • enhance my written English while I articulate my thoughts
  • keep a log of stuff I do
  • provide useful info for likeminded folks

Since I’m still a very curious person, the content will cover a wide variety of topics that interest me. Expect no consistency.

See you around!
